354	\begin{lemma}[The inclusion and projection are chain maps]
355	  \label{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}
356	  \lean{CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodInl, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSnd, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSplitting, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES_shortExact,
357	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeMid, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES,
358	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES_splitting, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeι,
359	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeι_f_zero, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.mono_horseshoeβ,
360	    CategoryTheory.twistedBiprod, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprod_X, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprod_d,
361	    CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodD, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodD_fst, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodD_snd,
362	    CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodInl_f, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSnd_f, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodInl_comp_Snd}
363	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_dComp}
364	  With \(d_B\) as in Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}, the degreewise biproduct
365	  coprojection \(\iota^n = \iota_{I_A^n} : I_A^n \to I_B^n\) and projection
366	  \(\pi^n = \pi_{I_C^n} : I_B^n \to I_C^n\) assemble into chain maps
367	  \(\iota : I_A^\bullet \to I_B^\bullet\) and \(\pi : I_B^\bullet \to I_C^\bullet\); that
368	  is, \(d_B^n \circ \iota^n = \iota^{n+1} \circ d_A^n\) and
369	  \(\pi^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = d_C^n \circ \pi^n\). Moreover \(\iota, \pi\) are compatible
370	  with the augmentations and form, in each degree, the split short exact sequence
371	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_B^n \to I_C^n \to 0\) of
372	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}.
373	\end{lemma}
374	
375	\begin{proof}
376	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_dComp, lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
377	  These are direct biproduct computations from the matrix form of \(d_B\). Pre-composing
378	  \(d_B^n\) with the coprojection \(\iota^n\) of the first summand selects the first
379	  column \(\begin{pmatrix} d_A^n \\ 0\end{pmatrix}\), which equals
380	  \(\iota^{n+1} \circ d_A^n\); so \(\iota\) is a chain map. Post-composing \(d_B^n\) with
381	  the projection \(\pi^{n+1}\) onto the second summand selects the second row
382	  \(\begin{pmatrix} 0 & d_C^n\end{pmatrix}\), which equals \(d_C^n \circ \pi^n\); so
383	  \(\pi\) is a chain map. Compatibility with the augmentations is the corresponding
384	  statement in degree \(-1 \to 0\), immediate from the construction of \(\beta\) in
385	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}. In each degree \(\iota^n = \iota_{I_A^n}\) and
386	  \(\pi^n = \pi_{I_C^n}\) are the structure maps of the biproduct, so the degree is split
387	  exact by Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}.
388	\end{proof}

390	\begin{lemma}\leanok
391	[Middle-term quasi-isomorphism transfer]
392	  \label{lem:quasiIso_tau2}
393	  \lean{HomologicalComplex.HomologySequence.quasiIso_τ₂}
394	  \uses{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
395	  Project-local supplement (the \(\tau_2\) companion of Mathlib's \(\tau_3\) transfer
396	  \texttt{quasiIso\_τ₃}). Let \(\varphi : S_1 \to S_2\) be a morphism of short exact
397	  sequences of cochain complexes, with components \(\varphi.\tau_1, \varphi.\tau_2,
398	  \varphi.\tau_3\) on the three columns. Suppose \(\varphi.\tau_1\) and \(\varphi.\tau_3\)
399	  are quasi-isomorphisms, and that the middle component \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a
400	  monomorphism in every degree \(i\) that has no incoming differential, and an
401	  epimorphism in every degree \(i\) that has no outgoing differential. Then
402	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a quasi-isomorphism.
403	\end{lemma}
404	
405	\begin{proof}
406	  \uses{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
407	  \leanok
408	  This is the homology four-lemma. Fix a degree \(i\); we show
409	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) is an isomorphism by proving it is both epimorphic and
410	  monomorphic and concluding that a map which is both is an isomorphism. \emph{Epi:}
411	  when \(i\) has an outgoing differential to some \(j\), the two short exact sequences
412	  yield a commuting ladder whose rows are the five-term exact windows of the homology
413	  long exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) about degree
414	  \(i\); the four-lemma (epi form), using that \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_1)\),
415	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_3)\) are epi and \(H^j(\varphi.\tau_1)\) is mono, forces
416	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) epi. When \(i\) has no outgoing differential,
417	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is epi in degree \(i\) by hypothesis, and an epimorphism of
418	  complexes that is epi in a degree with no outgoing differential is epi on homology
419	  there. \emph{Mono:} dually, when \(i\) has an incoming differential from some \(k\),
420	  the four-lemma (mono form), using that \(H^k(\varphi.\tau_3)\) is epi and
421	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_1), H^i(\varphi.\tau_3)\) are mono, forces
422	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) mono; when \(i\) has no incoming differential it is mono in
423	  that degree by hypothesis, hence mono on homology. Being epi and mono in every
424	  degree, \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), so
425	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a quasi-isomorphism.
426	\end{proof}

428	\begin{lemma}[The middle complex resolves \(B\)]
429	  \label{lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle}

437	  augmentation \(\beta : B \to I_B^0\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, is an injective
438	  resolution of \(B\): its terms are injective

440	  \(H^0 \cong B\) via \(\beta\).

446	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_chainMap} the maps \(\iota, \pi\) form, degreewise, the
447	  split short exact sequence \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_B^n \to I_C^n \to 0\); assembling these
448	  splittings (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}) gives a short exact sequence of
449	  cochain complexes

451	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^\bullet \longrightarrow I_B^\bullet
452	      \longrightarrow I_C^\bullet \longrightarrow 0 .
453	  \]

455	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}). The outer complexes \(I_A^\bullet\),
456	  \(I_C^\bullet\) are injective resolutions, hence exact in every positive degree
457	  (\(H^{k}(I_A) = H^{k}(I_C) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\)) with \(H^0(I_A) \cong A\),

467	\begin{lemma}\leanok
468	[Horseshoe lift of a short exact sequence to injective resolutions]
469	  \label{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}

473	    lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle,

475	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives and let
476	  \[
477	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0
478	  \]
479	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\). Then there exist injective
480	  resolutions \(I_A\) of \(A\), \(I_B\) of \(B\), \(I_C\) of \(C\), together with a
481	  short exact sequence of cochain complexes
482	  \[
483	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_B \longrightarrow I_C \longrightarrow 0
484	  \]
485	  which is compatible with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\), \(B \to I_B^0\),
486	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and which is \emph{degreewise split}: for every \(n\) the sequence
487	  \[
488	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n
489	      \longrightarrow 0
490	  \]
491	  is a split short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{A}\), so that
492	  \(I_B^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\).
493	\end{lemma}
494	
495	\begin{proof}
496	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:horseshoe_degree_split,
497	    lem:horseshoe_stage_mono, lem:horseshoe_twist, lem:horseshoe_dComp,
498	    lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle,
499	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
500	  \leanok
501	  This is the (dual) Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to
502	  Homological Algebra}, Lemma 2.2.8), assembled from the sub-lemmas of \S``The horseshoe
503	  construction, step by step''. Take \(I_A, I_C\) and set \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\):
504	  injective by Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}, degreewise split by
505	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}. The twist \(\tau\) and augmentation exist with
506	  the cocycle identity (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, via
507	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}); the matrix differential then squares to zero
508	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}) with coprojection and projection chain maps
509	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}); and \(I_B^\bullet\) resolves \(B\)
510	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle}). These data are exactly the asserted
511	  degreewise-split short exact sequence \(0 \to I_A \to I_B \to I_C \to 0\) of cochain
512	  complexes compatible with the augmentations.
513	\end{proof}
514	
515	\section{The dimension-shift step}
516	
517	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
518	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
519	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
520	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
521	complex-level homology long exact sequence
522	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
523	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We first record the shift at the
524	\emph{resolution} level — where the horseshoe lift directly supplies the
525	degreewise-split short exact sequence of injective resolutions — and then specialize
526	to objects.
527	
528	\begin{lemma}[Resolution-level dimension shift]
529	  \label{lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
530	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES, CategoryTheory.shortExact_of_degreewise_splitting, CategoryTheory.shortExact_map_mapHomologicalComplex_of_degreewise_splitting, CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_homology_mapHomologicalComplex_of_isRightAcyclic}
531	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
532	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
533	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
534	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\) be a short exact sequence with \(J\) right-\(G\)-acyclic,
535	  lifted to a \emph{degreewise-split} short exact sequence of injective resolutions
536	  \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\) (chain maps \(\varphi, \psi\) with
537	  \(\psi \circ \varphi = 0\) and a splitting of \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\)
538	  in each degree \(n\)). Then for every \(k \geq 0\) the connecting map of the homology
539	  long exact sequence of \(G(I_\bullet)\) is an isomorphism
540	  \[
541	    (R^{k+1} G)(Z) \;\cong\; (R^{k+2} G)(A).
542	  \]
543	\end{lemma}
544	
545	\begin{proof}
546	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
547	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
548	  Applying \(G\) degreewise to the degreewise-split sequence preserves each split
549	  degree, yielding a short exact sequence of cochain complexes
550	  \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in \(\mathcal{B}\). Its homology long
551	  exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) has connecting maps
552	  \(\delta^m : H^m(G(I_Z)) \to H^{m+1}(G(I_A))\); transport these across the
553	  injective-resolution computation \(H^m(G(I_-)) \cong (R^m G)(-)\)
554	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). For \(m = k+1 \geq 1\) the two
555	  homology objects flanking \(\delta^m\) in the long exact sequence both sit on the
556	  middle complex \(G(I_J)\), namely \(H^{k+1}(G(I_J))\) and \(H^{k+2}(G(I_J))\); both
557	  vanish because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}).
558	  With both neighbours zero, \(\delta^{k+1}\) is an isomorphism, giving
559	  \((R^{k+1} G)(Z) \cong (R^{k+2} G)(A)\).
560	\end{proof}
561	
562	\begin{lemma}\leanok
563	[Dimension shift across an acyclic short exact sequence]
564	  \label{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
565	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
566	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
567	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
568	    lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
569	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
570	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
571	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
572	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
573	  % between abelian categories and assume
574	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
575	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
576	  % of $\mathcal{A}$.
577	  % \begin{enumerate}
578	  % \item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
579	  % \item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
580	  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
581	  % surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
582	  % \end{enumerate}
583	  % In each of the three cases
584	  % $$
585	  % 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
586	  % $$
587	  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
588	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
589	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
590	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
591	  \[
592	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
593	  \]
594	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
595	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of
596	  right-derived functors furnish isomorphisms
597	  \[
598	    (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
599	    \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
600	  \]
601	  (The companion lowest-degree statement
602	  \((R^{1} G)(A) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J) \to G(Z))\) is isolated as the
603	  sibling Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
604	\end{lemma}
605	
606	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
607	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
608	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
609	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
610	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
611	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
612	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
613	\begin{proof}
614	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
615	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
616	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
617	    \leanok
618	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
619	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
620	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
621	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
622	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
623	
624	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
625	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
626	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
627	  complexes
628	  \[
629	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
630	  \]
631	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
632	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
633	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
634	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
635	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
636	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
637	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
638	  \[
639	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
640	      \longrightarrow 0
641	  \]
642	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
643	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
644	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
645	  \[
646	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
647	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
648	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
649	  \]
650	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
651	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
652	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
653	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
654	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
655	  sequence of right-derived functors
656	  \[
657	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
658	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
659	  \]
660	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
661	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
662	  \[
663	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
664	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
665	  \]
666	
667	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
668	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
669	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
670	  \[
671	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
672	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
673	  \]
674	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
675	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism, which is the
676	  asserted dimension shift. (The lowest-degree segment of the same long exact
677	  sequence yields the cokernel description of \((R^1 G)(A)\); that reading is
678	  recorded separately in Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
679	\end{proof}
680

683	  \label{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}

698	  % (Lemma \ref{lemma-left-exact-higher-derived})
699	  % we obtain an exact cohomology sequence

703	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
704	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
705	  \[
706	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
707	  \]

709	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)

717	\begin{proof}
718	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
719	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
720	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}

722	  This reads off the lowest-degree segment of the very long exact sequence of
723	  right-derived functors built, from the same short exact sequence
724	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\), in the proof of
725	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}; here we use only its initial terms, so we
726	  recall the construction briefly and then specialize. By the horseshoe lift
727	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
728	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a degreewise-split short exact
729	  sequence of cochain complexes \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\). Applying the
730	  additive functor \(G\) degreewise preserves each split degree, giving a short exact
731	  sequence of complexes \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in
732	  \(\mathcal{B}\); its complex-level homology long exact sequence
733	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}), transported across the
734	  injective-resolution computation \(H^k(G(I_\bullet)) \cong (R^k G)(-)\)
735	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}) and using
736	  \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}), has low-degree
737	  initial segment
738	  \[
739	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J).
740	  \]

742	  end \((R^1 G)(J)\) vanishes. Hence the segment
743	  \(G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to 0\) is exact with vanishing

750	\section{Cosyzygies of an acyclic resolution}
751	
752	The comparison theorem decomposes an acyclic resolution into the short exact
753	sequences cut out by its cosyzygies, and reads the cohomology of the applied complex
754	off those sequences using left-exactness of \(G\). We isolate the two ingredients
755	here.
756	
757	\begin{lemma}[Cosyzygy short exact sequences of an acyclic resolution]
758	  \label{lem:cosyzygy_ses}
759	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortExact, CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortComplex, CategoryTheory.cosyzygyKernelFork, CategoryTheory.cosyzygy_iCycles_comp_toCycles, CategoryTheory.epi_toCycles_of_exactAt}
760	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
761	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
762	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
763	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838).
764	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
765	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
766	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
767	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
768	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
769	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
770	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
771	  \[
772	    0 \longrightarrow A
773	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
774	      \longrightarrow \cdots
775	  \]
776	  be an exact resolution of \(A\) with every \(J^n\) right-\(G\)-acyclic. Define the
777	  cosyzygies \(Z^0 := A\) and, for \(m \geq 1\),
778	  \[
779	    Z^m \;:=\; \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr)
780	      \;=\; \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr).
781	  \]
782	  Then for every \(m \geq 0\) the sequence
783	  \[
784	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
785	      \longrightarrow 0 \tag{$S_m$}
786	  \]
787	  is short exact, with the inclusion \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) the canonical one,
788	  the surjection \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) the corestriction of
789	  \(J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image, and the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic.
790	  In particular \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\).
791	\end{lemma}
792	
793	\begin{proof}
794	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
795	  Fix \(m \geq 0\). Mono of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) is immediate: \(Z^m\) is by
796	  definition a subobject of \(J^m\) (for \(m = 0\) via the injection \(A \hookrightarrow
797	  J^0\) of the resolution, for \(m \geq 1\) as the kernel \(\ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\)).
798	  The map \(J^m \to Z^{m+1}\) is the corestriction of the differential
799	  \(d^m : J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image \(Z^{m+1} = \operatorname{im}(d^m)\), hence
800	  an epimorphism. Exactness in the middle is exactness of the resolution at \(J^m\):
801	  the image of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) equals \(\ker(d^m)\) --- for \(m \geq 1\)
802	  by the definition \(Z^m = \ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\), and for \(m = 0\) because
803	  exactness of \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to J^1\) makes \(A \hookrightarrow J^0\) identify
804	  \(A\) with \(\ker(d^0)\) --- while the kernel of the epimorphism
805	  \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) is exactly \(\ker(d^m)\). Thus
806	  \(\operatorname{im}(Z^m \to J^m) = \ker(J^m \to Z^{m+1})\), so \((S_m)\) is short
807	  exact. The middle term \(J^m\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis. The final
808	  identity \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\)
809	  is the case \(m = 0\): \(\operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0) = \operatorname{coker}(Z^0 \to J^0)
810	  \cong Z^1\) by \((S_0)\).
811	\end{proof}
812	
813	\begin{lemma}[Applied cosyzygy is the cocycle object of the applied complex]
814	  \label{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}
815	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_hom_iCycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_toCycles}
816	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
817	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
818	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D) — the left-exact initial segment
819	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C)$ specialized to the cosyzygy sequences
820	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
821	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
822	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots$
823	  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
824	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
825	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}), left-exact initial segment.}
826	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
827	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, for every \(n \geq 0\) the morphism \(G\) applies to
828	  the inclusion \(Z^n \hookrightarrow J^n\) to give an isomorphism
829	  \[
830	    G(Z^n) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr),
831	  \]
832	  identifying \(G(Z^n)\), as a subobject of \(G(J^n)\), with the \(n\)-th cocycle
833	  object of the applied complex \(G(J^\bullet)\). For \(n = 0\) this reads
834	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)\).
835	\end{lemma}
836	
837	\begin{proof}
838	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
839	  Fix \(n \geq 0\). Apply the left-exact functor \(G\) to the cosyzygy short exact
840	  sequence \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\) of
841	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}; left-exactness yields an exact sequence
842	  \[
843	    0 \longrightarrow G(Z^n) \longrightarrow G(J^n) \longrightarrow G(Z^{n+1}),
844	  \]
845	  so \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) is the kernel of \(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})\).
846	  Next, the differential \(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\) of the applied complex factors as
847	  \(G(J^n) \xrightarrow{G(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1})} G(Z^{n+1})
848	  \xrightarrow{G(Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1})} G(J^{n+1})\), because the original
849	  differential \(J^n \to J^{n+1}\) factors through its image \(Z^{n+1}\) as
850	  \(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1}\) and \(G\) preserves
851	  composition. The second factor \(G(Z^{n+1}) \to G(J^{n+1})\) is a monomorphism (the
852	  image of the left-exact application of \(G\) to \((S_{n+1})\), whose first map
853	  \(G(Z^{n+1}) \hookrightarrow G(J^{n+1})\) is mono). A monomorphism does not change
854	  kernels under precomposition, so
855	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) = \ker(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})) = G(Z^n)\). Hence
856	  \(G(Z^n) \xrightarrow{\cong} \ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\) as subobjects of
857	  \(G(J^n)\). For \(n = 0\), \(Z^0 = A\) gives
858	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0(G(J^\bullet))\), the last equality being
859	  the definition of the zeroth cohomology of a cochain complex starting in degree
860	  \(0\) (no incoming differential). The use of \(R^0 G \cong G\)
861	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) is what licenses reading \(G(A)\) as
862	  \((R^0 G)(A)\) when this identification feeds the comparison theorem.
863	\end{proof}

877	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break

882	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}) and Tag 015D
883	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
884	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
885	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, the cohomology of the applied complex
886	  \(G(J^\bullet) = \bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to \cdots\bigr)\) is computed by
887	  \[
888	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\; G(A),
889	    \qquad
890	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\;
891	      \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr) \quad (n \geq 1),
892	  \]
893	  where \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) is \(G\) applied to the surjection

897	\begin{proof}
898	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
899	  The degree-zero statement is the \(n = 0\) case of
900	  Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}: \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1))
901	  \cong G(Z^0) = G(A)\). Fix now \(n \geq 1\). By definition of the cohomology of the
902	  cochain complex \(G(J^\bullet)\) in positive degree,
903	  \[
904	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)
905	      \;=\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr) \big/
906	            \operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr).
907	  \]
908	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles} the numerator is
909	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) \cong G(Z^n)\), realized as the subobject
910	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\). Under this identification the differential
911	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\) lands inside \(G(Z^n)\): it factors as
912	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \xrightarrow{G(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n)} G(Z^n)
913	  \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) by the factorization of \(J^{n-1} \to J^n\) through its image
914	  \(Z^n\), so its image equals the image of \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) inside the
915	  subobject \(G(Z^n)\). Therefore the quotient
916	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n))\) is exactly
917	  \(G(Z^n) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) =
918	  \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\). Hence
919	  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\).
920	\end{proof}

1022	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)

2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}

4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, definition of $R^i f_*$ as the

8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter records the derived-functor higher direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\)
11	for \(i \geq 1\). It provides the single object that the {\v C}ech computation of
12	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} compares against: the comparison
13	theorem \(\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}\) identifies the cohomology of the
14	relative {\v C}ech complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) wherever the derived functor
15	is defined.
16	
17	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category).
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
19	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	
21	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
22	
23	\begin{definition}\leanok
24	[Higher direct image]
25	  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
26	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
27	  % NOTE (iter-233): the Lean `higherDirectImage` carries an explicit instance
28	  % hypothesis `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`, because Mathlib has
29	  % `Abelian X.Modules` but NOT `EnoughInjectives` / `IsGrothendieckAbelian` for
30	  % `SheafOfModules` over a sheaf of rings (the Grothendieck-abelian instance is
31	  % only for `Sheaf J A` with a fixed value category). The definition is therefore
32	  % conditional; the hypothesis can be dropped once a lane proves that instance.
33	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
34	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing' (proof), describing $R^i f_*$ as the
35	  % $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ via the sheafified presheaf
36	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$
37	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L188--192).
38	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "According to
39	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
40	  % the sheaf
41	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
42	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$."
43	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
44	  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
45	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
46	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
47	  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
48	  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
49	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
50	  sheafification of the presheaf
51	  \[
52	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^i\bigl(f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
53	    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
54	  \]
55	  where \(H^i(-, -)\) denotes sheaf cohomology. For \(i = 0\) this recovers the
56	  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
57	\end{definition}

3900	\begin{lemma}[The forgetful functor reflects isomorphisms]
3901	  \label{lem:forget_reflectsIso_mathlib}
3902	  \lean{SheafOfModules.fullyFaithfulForget}
3903	  \mathlibok
3904	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3905	  The forgetful functor \(\operatorname{forget}\) from sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to the
3906	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups is fully faithful, hence reflects isomorphisms; the same holds
3907	  for \(\operatorname{toSheaf}\). Consequently a morphism of sheaves of modules is an isomorphism as
3908	  soon as its underlying morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is, and — since a morphism of sheaves
3909	  which is an isomorphism on every member of a basis of the topology is an isomorphism — it suffices
3910	  to check \(\operatorname{fromTilde\Gamma}\) on the basis of distinguished opens \(\{D(f)\}\).

3950	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and let \(f \in R\). Then the restriction-of-sections map
3951	  \(\rho_f : \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\) exhibits
3952	  \(\Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\) at the powers of
3953	  \(f\): one has \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, \rho_f\). This is the
3954	  generalization of Tag 01HV~(4) (\(\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde{M}) = M_f\)) from \(\widetilde{M}\) to an
3955	  arbitrary quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\), and is the single load-bearing input of Route B.

3957	\begin{proof}
3958	  \uses{lem:isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover, lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_subcover,
3959	    lem:free_isQuasicoherent, lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
3960	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_subcover}, refine the quasi-coherence cover of
3961	  \(\mathcal{F}\) to a finite standard cover \(\operatorname{Spec} R = \bigcup_{j=1}^{n} D(g_j)\) with
3962	  \(\operatorname{span}\{g_1, \ldots, g_n\} = R\), chosen so that on each \(D(g_j)\) the
3963	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) is presented as a cokernel of free \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules
3964	  \(\mathcal{O}^{(J_j)} \to \mathcal{O}^{(I_j)} \to \mathcal{F}|_{D(g_j)} \to 0\) (the local
3965	  presentation carried by the quasi-coherence datum of \(\mathcal{F}\)).
3966	
3967	  Fix \(j\) and localize the situation at \(g_j\). For a free \(\mathcal{O}\)-module the
3968	  section-restriction over a distinguished open is the structure-sheaf localization
3969	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:free_isQuasicoherent}: free \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules are
3970	  \(\widetilde{(\text{free})}\), and \(\widetilde{(-)}.\operatorname{toOpen}\) is an
3971	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) at the powers of the defining element). The section functor
3972	  \(\Gamma(D(g_j), -)\) is right-exact on the standard cover — its failure to be exact in positive
3973	  degrees is measured by the {\v C}ech complex, which is acyclic for quasi-coherent (in particular
3974	  free) coefficients on a standard cover (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}). Hence the cokernel
3975	  presentation is preserved by taking sections and by further localizing at \(f\); comparing the two
3976	  presentations shows that, after localizing at \(g_j\), the section-restriction map \(\rho_f\)
3977	  becomes the localization map of the local module \(\Gamma(D(g_j), \mathcal{F})\) at the powers of
3978	  \(f\). In other words the localized map
3979	  \((\rho_f)_{g_j} : \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_{g_j} \to \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})_{g_j}\) satisfies
3980	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\,(\rho_f)_{g_j}\).
3981	
3982	  Now apply Lemma~\ref{lem:isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover} with
3983	  \(M = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\), \(N = \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\), the map \(\rho_f\), the element
3984	  \(f\), and the spanning family \(g_1, \ldots, g_n\): since each localized map \((\rho_f)_{g_j}\) is
3985	  an \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) for the powers of \(f\) and the \(g_j\) generate the unit
3986	  ideal, the span-cover descent concludes that \(\rho_f\) itself is an
3987	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) for \(\{1, f, f^2, \ldots\}\).
3988	\end{proof}

4125	\begin{lemma}\leanok
4126	[Free \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules are quasi-coherent]
4127	  \label{lem:free_isQuasicoherent}
4128	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.free_isQuasicoherent}
4129	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma
4130	  % \texttt{lemma-colimit-quasi-coherent} (read from
4131	  % references/stacks-schemes.tex, L1434--1438).

4134	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-colimit-quasi-coherent}.}
4135	  Let \(X = \operatorname{Spec} R\). For any index type \(\iota\) the free
4136	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{O}_X^{(\iota)}\) on \(\iota\) generators is
4137	  quasi-coherent. Indeed it is the sheaf \(\widetilde{R^{(\iota)}}\) associated to the
4138	  free \(R\)-module \(R^{(\iota)} = \bigoplus_{\iota} R\) (the finitely-supported
4139	  functions \(\iota \to R\)): the tilde functor carries \(R^{(\iota)}\) to a direct sum
4140	  of copies of the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X = \widetilde{R}\), and every sheaf of
4141	  the form \(\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent. Quasi-coherence is preserved under the
4142	  isomorphism \(\mathcal{O}_X^{(\iota)} \cong \widetilde{R^{(\iota)}}\); concretely it is
4143	  obtained from the identification of the free \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module with the
4144	  tilde of the free \(R\)-module.

4147	\begin{lemma}\leanok
4148	[Finite basic-open refinement of a cover of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\)]
4149	  \label{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_subcover}
4150	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.exists_finite_basicOpen_subcover}
4151	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, \texttt{lemma-standard-open} (parts 2--3) and
4152	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine} (proof) (read from
4153	  % references/stacks-schemes.tex, L531--534, L1289--1301).
4154	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\item Any open covering of $D(f)$ can be refined to a finite
4155	  % open covering of the form $D(f) = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(g_i)$.
4156	  % \item If $g_1, \ldots, g_n \in R$, then $D(f) \subset \bigcup D(g_i)$
4157	  % if and only if $g_1, \ldots, g_n$ generate the unit ideal in $R_f$."
4158	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Since every standard open $D(f)$ is quasi-compact we see that
4159	  % $X$ is locally quasi-compact, i.e., every point has a fundamental
4160	  % system of quasi-compact neighbourhoods ...
4161	  % In other words, we get an open covering by $U$'s with this property.
4162	  % By Lemma \ref{lemma-standard-open} for example we can refine this
4163	  % covering to  a standard open covering."
4164	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-standard-open} and
4165	  \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine} (proof).}
4166	  Let \(R\) be a ring and \((U_i)_{i \in \iota}\) a family of opens of
4167	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) with \(\bigsqcup_i U_i = \operatorname{Spec} R\) (i.e.\
4168	  \(\bigcup_i U_i = \top\)). Then there exist finitely many elements
4169	  \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in R\) and indices \(\varphi : \{1, \ldots, n\} \to \iota\) such
4170	  that each basic open satisfies \(D(f_j) \subseteq U_{\varphi(j)}\) and the \(f_j\)
4171	  generate the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{span}\{f_1, \ldots, f_n\} = R\) (equivalently
4172	  \(\bigcup_j D(f_j) = \operatorname{Spec} R\)).
4173	\end{lemma}
4174	\begin{proof}
4175	
4176	\leanok
4177	  The basic opens \(D(f)\), \(f \in R\), form a basis of the Zariski topology on
4178	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R\); hence every \(U_i\) is a union of basic opens, and refining
4179	  the cover \((U_i)\) yields a cover of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) by basic opens
4180	  \(D(f)\), each contained in some \(U_{\varphi}\). The space \(\operatorname{Spec} R\)
4181	  is quasi-compact, so finitely many of these basic opens \(D(f_1), \ldots, D(f_n)\)
4182	  already cover \(\operatorname{Spec} R\), with \(D(f_j) \subseteq U_{\varphi(j)}\) by
4183	  construction. Finally a finite family of basic opens covers all of
4184	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) if and only if the \(f_j\) generate the unit ideal:
4185	  \(\bigcup_j D(f_j) = \operatorname{Spec} R \iff \operatorname{span}\{f_j\} = R\), since
4186	  the closed complement \(V(\operatorname{span}\{f_j\})\) is empty exactly when the ideal
4187	  is the whole ring.
4188	\end{proof}

4325	  \(U \subseteq X\) be open, and suppose \(\mathcal{F}|_U\) admits a presentation

4340	  preserves cokernels and arbitrary direct sums of \(\mathcal{O}\); applying it to the given
4341	  right-exact sequence yields a right-exact sequence
4342	  \(\mathcal{O}_{D(g)}^{(J)} \to \mathcal{O}_{D(g)}^{(I)} \to \mathcal{F}|_{D(g)} \to 0\).

4402	\begin{lemma}[\(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) is local on a finite spanning cover]
4403	  \label{lem:isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover}
4404	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover,
4405	    AlgebraicGeometry.exists_sum_pow_eq_one, AlgebraicGeometry.mem_range_of_span_pow,
4406	    AlgebraicGeometry.eq_zero_of_span_pow, AlgebraicGeometry.map_smul_endFun,
4407	    AlgebraicGeometry.bump_eq, AlgebraicGeometry.per_j_surj, AlgebraicGeometry.per_j_eq}
4408	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(M\) and \(N\) be \(R\)-modules, let
4409	  \(g : M \to N\) be an \(R\)-linear map, let \(f \in R\), and let
4410	  \(s : \{1, \ldots, n\} \to R\) be a finite family with
4411	  \(\operatorname{span}\{s_1, \ldots, s_n\} = R\) (the unit ideal). For each \(j\) write
4412	  \(M_{s_j} = (\operatorname{powers} s_j)^{-1} M\) and \(N_{s_j} = (\operatorname{powers}
4413	  s_j)^{-1} N\) for the localisations at the powers of \(s_j\), and let
4414	  \(g_{s_j} : M_{s_j} \to N_{s_j}\) be the induced \(R\)-linear (equivalently
4415	  \(R_{s_j}\)-linear) map. Suppose that for every \(j\) the localised map \(g_{s_j}\)
4416	  exhibits \(N_{s_j}\) as the localisation of \(M_{s_j}\) at the powers of (the image of)
4417	  \(f\), i.e.\ \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g_{s_j}\). Then
4418	  \(g\) itself exhibits \(N\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the powers of \(f\):
4419	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g\).
4420	\end{lemma}
4421	\begin{proof}
4422	
4423	  This is a purely commutative-algebra statement: the predicate
4424	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g\) is the conjunction of its
4425	  three defining conditions, and each is verified by descent along the spanning cover. Throughout we use the standard partition
4426	  of unity: since \(\operatorname{span}\{s_j\} = R\), for any chosen exponents \(N_j\) the
4427	  powers \(s_j^{N_j}\) again span the unit ideal, so there are \(r_j \in R\) with
4428	  \(\sum_j r_j s_j^{N_j} = 1\); and an element of an \(R\)-module is zero iff its image in
4429	  \(M_{s_j}\) vanishes for every \(j\) (locality of being zero on a spanning cover, the
4430	  \(s_j\)-torsion form of the same partition of unity).
4431	
4432	  \emph{\(f\) acts invertibly on \(N\).} For each \(j\), the hypothesis
4433	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g_{s_j}\) makes multiplication
4434	  by \(f\) on \(N_{s_j}\) bijective. Multiplication by \(f\) on \(N\) is an isomorphism iff it
4435	  is so after localising at each \(s_j\) (an \(R\)-linear endomorphism is bijective iff it is
4436	  bijective on the cover \(\{D(s_j)\}\), since injectivity and surjectivity are both local on
4437	  a spanning cover); hence \(f \cdot (-) : N \to N\) is bijective, and likewise every power
4438	  \(f^k\).
4439	
4440	  \emph{Every \(y \in N\) satisfies \(f^k y = g(x)\) for some \(x \in M\), \(k\).}
4441	  Fix \(y \in N\). For each \(j\), surjectivity of \(g_{s_j}\) as a localisation at the powers
4442	  of \(f\) yields \(m_j \in M\), exponents \(a_j\) (clearing the \(s_j\)-denominator) and
4443	  \(k_j\) with \(s_j^{a_j} f^{k_j} y = g(m_j)\) holding after localising at \(s_j\); clearing
4444	  the remaining \(s_j\)-torsion (enlarging \(a_j\)) we may take this as an honest equality
4445	  \(s_j^{a_j} f^{k_j} y = g(m_j)\) in \(N\). Choose a common \(k = \max_j k_j\) (replacing
4446	  \(m_j\) by \(f^{k - k_j} m_j\)) so that \(s_j^{a_j} f^{k} y = g(m_j)\) for all \(j\). Pick,
4447	  by the partition of unity, \(r_j\) with \(\sum_j r_j s_j^{a_j} = 1\) (possible because the
4448	  \(s_j^{a_j}\) span the unit ideal). Then
4449	  \(f^{k} y = \sum_j r_j s_j^{a_j} f^{k} y = \sum_j r_j\, g(m_j) = g\bigl(\sum_j r_j m_j\bigr)\),
4450	  so \(x = \sum_j r_j m_j\) and the exponent \(k\) work.
4451	
4452	  \emph{If \(g(x) = g(x')\) then \(f^k(x - x') = 0\) for some \(k\).} Put
4453	  \(z = x - x'\), so \(g(z) = 0\). For each \(j\), the equaliser clause of
4454	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g_{s_j}\) gives, from the
4455	  vanishing of \(g_{s_j}(z) = 0\) in \(N_{s_j}\), an exponent \(k_j\) with
4456	  \(f^{k_j} z = 0\) after localising at \(s_j\); clearing the \(s_j\)-denominator there is
4457	  \(b_j\) with \(s_j^{b_j} f^{k_j} z = 0\) in \(M\). Take \(k = \max_j k_j\), so
4458	  \(s_j^{b_j} f^{k} z = 0\) for all \(j\). With \(r_j\) chosen so that
4459	  \(\sum_j r_j s_j^{b_j} = 1\),
4460	  \(f^{k} z = \sum_j r_j s_j^{b_j} f^{k} z = 0\). Hence \(f^{k}(x - x') = 0\).
4461	
4462	  The three clauses are exactly the data of
4463	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g\).
4464	\end{proof}

4466	\begin{lemma}[Localised sections of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
4467	  \label{lem:qcoh_localized_sections}
4468	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.qcoh_localized_sections}
4469	  \uses{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_subcover, lem:isQuasicoherent_restrict_basicOpen, lem:isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover}
4470	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, \texttt{lemma-widetilde-pullback} and the remark
4471	  % following it, with \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine} (proof) (read from
4472	  % references/stacks-schemes.tex, L1262--1276, L1287--1387).
4473	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The second assertion follows from the fact
4474	  % that $\psi^{-1}(D(f)) = D(\psi^\sharp(f))$ and hence
4475	  % $$
4476	  % \psi_* \widetilde N(D(f)) = \widetilde N(D(\psi^\sharp(f))) =
4477	  % N_{\psi^\sharp(f)} = (N_R)_f = \widetilde{N_R}(D(f))
4478	  % $$
4479	  % as desired."
4480	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma \ref{lemma-widetilde-pullback} above says in particular
4481	  % that if you restrict
4482	  % the sheaf $\widetilde M$ to a standard affine open subspace
4483	  % $D(f)$, then you get $\widetilde{M_f}$. We will use this from
4484	  % now on without further mention."
4485	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-widetilde-pullback}.}
4486	  Let \(X = \operatorname{Spec} R\), let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent
4487	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and let \(f \in R\). Then the restriction of sections
4488	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\) exhibits
4489	  \(\Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\) as the localisation of \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\) at the
4490	  powers of \(f\): it is an \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) for the submonoid
4491	  \(\{1, f, f^2, \ldots\}\). In particular every section over \(D(f)\) is, after
4492	  multiplication by a suitable power \(f^N\), the restriction of a global section, and a
4493	  global section restricting to \(0\) on \(D(f)\) is annihilated by a power of \(f\).
4494	\end{lemma}
4495	\begin{proof}
4496	  \uses{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_subcover, lem:isQuasicoherent_restrict_basicOpen, lem:isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover}
4497	  Choose, by Lemma~\ref{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_subcover}, a finite basic-open cover
4498	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R = \bigcup_{j=1}^{n} D(s_j)\) with
4499	  \(\operatorname{span}\{s_1, \ldots, s_n\} = R\). On each member
4500	  \(D(s_j) = \operatorname{Spec} R_{s_j}\) the restriction \(\mathcal{F}|_{D(s_j)}\) is, by
4501	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isQuasicoherent_restrict_basicOpen}, a quasi-coherent
4502	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R_{s_j}}\)-module admitting a presentation, hence (by the
4503	  affine structure theorem) \(\mathcal{F}|_{D(s_j)} \cong \widetilde{M_j}\) for an
4504	  \(R_{s_j}\)-module \(M_j = \Gamma(D(s_j), \mathcal{F})\). For the standard sheaf
4505	  \(\widetilde{M_j}\) the sections over a distinguished open \(D(g) \subseteq D(s_j)\) are
4506	  the away localisation \((M_j)_g\) (\texttt{lemma-widetilde-pullback}:
4507	  \(\Gamma(D(g), \widetilde{M}) = M_g\)). Applying this with \(g\) the image of \(f\) shows
4508	  that, after localising at \(s_j\), the restriction map
4509	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\) becomes the localisation map
4510	  \((M_j) \to (M_j)_f\), i.e.\ the localised map
4511	  \(g_{s_j} : \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_{s_j} \to \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})_{s_j}\) is an
4512	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) for the powers of \(f\).
4513	
4514	  Now apply Lemma~\ref{lem:isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover} with \(M = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\),
4515	  \(N = \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\), the restriction map \(g\), the element \(f\), and the
4516	  spanning family \(s_1, \ldots, s_n\): since \(g_{s_j}\) is an
4517	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) for the powers of \(f\) for every \(j\) and the
4518	  \(s_j\) generate the unit ideal, the patching primitive concludes that \(g\) itself is an
4519	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) for \(\{1, f, f^2, \ldots\}\). Concretely this packages
4520	  the two clauses: every section over \(D(f)\) is, after multiplication by some \(f^N\), the
4521	  restriction of a global section, and a global section vanishing on \(D(f)\) is annihilated
4522	  by a power of \(f\).
4523	\end{proof}

4533	  % the following argument: Let us construct an $R$-module map
4534	  % $$

4536	  % $$
4537	  % Namely, given $m \in M$ we get $m_i = m/1 \in M_{f_i} = M_i$
4538	  % by construction of $M$. By construction of $M_i$ this corresponds

4543	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then \(\mathcal{F}\) is generated by its global sections:

4551	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_subcover} pick a finite basic-open cover
4552	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R = \bigcup_{j=1}^{n} D(f_j)\) on each of which
4553	  \(\mathcal{F}|_{D(f_j)} \cong \widetilde{M_j}\). Each \(M_j\) is generated, as an
4554	  \(R_{f_j}\)-module, by local sections \(t \in \Gamma(D(f_j), \mathcal{F})\). By the
4555	  surjectivity half of Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_localized_sections}, after multiplying by a
4556	  suitable power \(f_j^{N}\) each such local section is the restriction of a global
4557	  section \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\); since \(f_j\) is a unit on \(D(f_j)\), these

4560	  \(\mathcal{F}\) on every member of the cover, hence everywhere: the induced map
4561	  \(\mathcal{O}_X^{(I)} \to \mathcal{F}\) is surjective on each \(D(f_j)\) and so is an
4562	  epimorphism of sheaves.

4611	\begin{proof}
4612	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (item~(7) and
4613	  proof), Tag 01HV.}
4614	  Kernels in the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules are computed
4615	  objectwise and sheafified, and isomorphisms of sheaves may be checked on stalks: a
4616	  morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is an isomorphism if and only if it induces an
4617	  isomorphism on every stalk. So it suffices to compare stalks. By the stalk computation
4618	  for the tilde functor (\texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} item~(7)) the stalk of
4619	  \(\widetilde{M}\) at a point \(x \in \operatorname{Spec} R\) corresponding to the prime
4620	  \(\mathfrak{p}\) is the localisation
4621	  \(\widetilde{M}_x = M_{\mathfrak{p}}\), naturally in \(M\): the stalk functor at \(x\)
4622	  applied to \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is the localisation functor \(M \mapsto M_{\mathfrak{p}}\).
4623	
4624	  Now let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be an \(R\)-module map with kernel
4625	  \(K = \operatorname{Ker}(\varphi)\), giving the exact sequence
4626	  \(0 \to K \to M \xrightarrow{\varphi} N\). Localisation \(R \to R_{\mathfrak{p}}\) is
4627	  flat, hence the functor \(M \mapsto M_{\mathfrak{p}}\) is exact and in particular
4628	  preserves kernels: \(K_{\mathfrak{p}} = \operatorname{Ker}(\varphi_{\mathfrak{p}} :
4629	  M_{\mathfrak{p}} \to N_{\mathfrak{p}})\). Translating through the stalk identification,
4630	  the natural comparison map
4631	  \(\widetilde{K} \to \operatorname{Ker}(\widetilde{\varphi})\) induces, at every point
4632	  \(x \leftrightarrow \mathfrak{p}\), the isomorphism \(K_{\mathfrak{p}} \xrightarrow{\sim}
4633	  \operatorname{Ker}(\varphi_{\mathfrak{p}})\) (using that the stalk functor, being a
4634	  filtered colimit, is itself exact and commutes with forming the kernel). Being a
4635	  stalkwise isomorphism, the comparison map is an isomorphism of sheaves, so
4636	  \(\operatorname{Ker}(\widetilde{\varphi}) = \widetilde{\operatorname{Ker}(\varphi)}\).
4637	  The same stalkwise-flatness argument applied to a finite diagram of \(R\)-modules shows
4638	  that \(\widetilde{(-)}\) carries finite limits to finite limits; equivalently
4639	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is left exact, i.e.\ preserves finite limits (kernels).

4642	  \begin{itemize}
4643	    \item \textbf{(A) The stalk comparison is \(R_{\mathfrak{p}}\)-linear, not merely
4644	      additive.} The stalk of \(\widetilde{M}\) at \(x\) is computed as a stalk of the
4645	      underlying sheaf of abelian groups, so the induced stalk map is \emph{a priori}
4646	      only a map of abelian groups. To know it is the localised \(R\)-module map
4647	      \(M_{\mathfrak{p}} \to N_{\mathfrak{p}}\) — and hence injective/exact by the
4648	      flatness of localisation — one identifies it on germs with the linear localisation
4649	      map: the germ \(\operatorname{germ}_x\) is \(R_{\mathfrak{p}}\)-linear (the linear
4650	      germ \(\operatorname{germ}_\ell\)), and the stalk action of the abelian-group stalk
4651	      functor on \(\widetilde{\varphi}\) agrees with \(\varphi\) on the image of the
4652	      structure map \(\widetilde{M} \to \widetilde{M}_x\). This germ-naturality identity
4653	      is the load-bearing step.
4654	      % NOTE: realized by the helper `tilde_stalkFunctor_map_toStalk` (the germ-naturality
4655	      % transport, axiom-clean iter-034) together with the public linear germ
4656	      % `StructureSheaf.germₗ` and the Γ-linearity of `Scheme.Modules.Hom.app`; this packages
4657	      % the abelian-group stalk map `σ_x` as the R-linear localisation map and identifies it
4658	      % with `IsLocalizedModule.map …`, whose injectivity is the already-built
4659	      % `tilde_toStalk_map_injective`.

4661	      morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is an isomorphism on every
4662	      stalk is an isomorphism; equivalently the family of stalk functors jointly reflects
4663	      isomorphisms (and jointly reflects the finite limits being compared). One checks the
4664	      kernel-comparison map is a stalk-wise isomorphism by (A), then concludes it is an
4665	      isomorphism of sheaves of modules.
4666	      % NOTE: in Lean, reduce to the underlying abelian-presheaf comparison and use the
4667	      % jointly-reflecting stalk family on a sheaf (e.g. `JointlyReflectIsomorphisms` /
4668	      % `Sheaf.forget ⋙ stalkFunctor x` preserving finite limits), via

4670	      % the ModuleCat-valued stalk path is DEAD (Mathlib privacy of `toStalkₗ'`/`stalkIsoₗ`).
4671	    \item \textbf{(C) Reduction to the underlying abelian-presheaf composite.} The
4672	      finite-limit-preservation question for \(\widetilde{(-)}\) into sheaves of modules is
4673	      equivalent to the same question for the composite of \(\widetilde{(-)}\) with the
4674	      forgetful functor to the underlying presheaf of abelian groups, where the

4708	  hypothesis. On each member \(D(f_j)\) of a finite trivialising cover both source and
4709	  target are of the form \(\widetilde{(-)}\), and the map \(\sigma.\pi\) corresponds to an

4812	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, lem:qcoh_iso_tilde_sections, def:affine_cover_system,
4813	    def:has_vanishing_higher_cech}
4814	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KG, Lemma
4815	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} (proof, condition (3)) (read
4816	  % from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L172--173).
4817	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Finally, condition (3) of the lemma follows from
4818	  % Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}."
4819	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KG
4820	  (\texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}, proof).}
4821	  Let \(X = \operatorname{Spec} R\) be an affine scheme and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
4822	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then \(\mathcal{F}\) has vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for the
4823	  affine cover system (Definition~\ref{def:affine_cover_system},
4824	  Definition~\ref{def:has_vanishing_higher_cech}): for every standard cover \(\mathcal{U}\) and every
4825	  \(p > 0\) one has \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\). This is the seed that supplies
4826	  condition (3) of the basis-comparison Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}, and is the 01I8
4827	  globalisation of the tilde-case standard-cover vanishing.
4828	\end{lemma}
4829	\begin{proof}
4830	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, lem:qcoh_iso_tilde_sections}
4831	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_iso_tilde_sections} the quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) is isomorphic to
4832	  \(\widetilde{M}\) for \(M = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\), with sections over each face \(D(g_\sigma)\)
4833	  the away localisation \(M_{g_\sigma}\). The standard-cover {\v C}ech vanishing of
4834	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} applies to the tilde sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\): its section
4835	  {\v C}ech complex over any standard cover is exact in positive degrees. Transporting along the
4836	  isomorphism \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{M}\), which is natural in the coefficient and so induces
4837	  an isomorphism of section {\v C}ech complexes, gives \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\)
4838	  for all \(p > 0\) and every standard cover \(\mathcal{U}\). Hence \(\mathcal{F}\) has vanishing
4839	  higher {\v C}ech cohomology for the affine cover system.
4840	\end{proof}
4841	
4842	The {\v C}ech-to-cohomology comparison on a basis
4843	(Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}) is proved by a dimension shift on the
4844	coefficient module, decomposed below into four sub-lemmas: the short exact sequence of
4845	{\v C}ech complexes coming from a basis (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_ses_of_basis}), the
4846	resulting stability of vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology under the injective
4847	quotient (Lemma~\ref{lem:quotient_vanishing_cech}), the sheaf-cohomology base case
4848	\(H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_one_vanishing}), and the
4849	dimension-shift induction \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\)
4850	(Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_pos_vanishing}). The top lemma's proof then just
4851	chains these.
4852	
4853	\subsection{Section {\v C}ech infrastructure and the cover-system encoding}
4854	
4855	The sub-lemmas below are formalized in a \emph{cover-local}, presheaf-level, hypothesis-driven
4856	form: each lemma fixes a single index family \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) and works with the
4857	section {\v C}ech complexes of that one covering, taking the geometric inputs (section-level
4858	exactness on faces, positive-degree {\v C}ech vanishing) as hypotheses. The cover-global
4859	\((\mathcal{B}, \mathrm{Cov})\) data of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis} re-enters only
4860	in the inductive step (Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_pos_vanishing}), where it is packaged by
4861	the cover-system encoding (Definition~\ref{def:basis_cov_system},
4862	Definition~\ref{def:has_vanishing_higher_cech}) and discharges those hypotheses via the per-face
4863	short-exact-sequence derivation (Lemma~\ref{lem:face_ses_of_sheaf_ses}). The supporting
4864	definitions and the degreewise product engine are collected first.
4865	
4866	\begin{definition}
4867	
4868	\leanok
4869	[{\v C}ech cohomology accessor]
4870	  \label{def:cech_cohomology_accessor}
4871	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechCohomology}
4872	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex}
4873	  \textit{Project-local accessor.}
4874	  For an index family \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) (giving the covering \(\mathcal{U}\)), a
4875	  presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{F}\), and \(p \geq 0\), the \emph{{\v C}ech
4876	  cohomology} \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) is the degree-\(p\) homology of the section
4877	  {\v C}ech complex of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex},
4878	  \[
4879	    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \;:=\;
4880	      H^p\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr),
4881	  \]
4882	  taken as an object of \(\mathrm{Ab}\). This is the named wrapper that the 01EO chain refers to
4883	  uniformly.
4884	\end{definition}
4885	
4886	\begin{definition}
4887	[Functoriality of the section {\v C}ech complex]
4888	  \label{def:section_cech_functoriality}
4889	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechCosimplicialMap,
4890	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechCosimplicialFunctor,
4891	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechComplexFunctor,
4892	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechComplexMap}
4893	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex}
4894	  \textit{Project-local.}

4895	  A morphism \(\varphi : \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G}\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
4896	  acts on the section {\v C}ech data of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} coordinatewise:
4897	  on the basic-open section group of each intersection it is the map
4898	  \(\varphi(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}) : \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}) \to \mathcal{G}(U_{i_0 \ldots
4899	  i_p})\). These assemble into a cosimplicial morphism (the \emph{section {\v C}ech cosimplicial
4900	  map}), functorial in \(\varphi\) (the \emph{section {\v C}ech cosimplicial functor}). Composing
4901	  with the alternating-coface-map complex functor packages the section {\v C}ech complex as a
4902	  functor in the coefficient presheaf (the \emph{section {\v C}ech complex functor}) and yields,
4903	  for each \(\varphi\), the induced chain map of section {\v C}ech complexes
4904	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \varphi)\). These are the maps used to build the short
4905	  complex of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_short_complex}.
4906	\end{definition}
4907	
4908	\begin{definition}
4909	
4910	\leanok
4911	[Per-face section short complex]
4912	  \label{def:face_short_complex}
4913	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.faceShortComplex}
4914	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex}
4915	  \textit{Project-local building block.}
4916	  Let \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) be an index family and let
4917	  \(P : \mathcal{P}_1 \to \mathcal{P}_2 \to \mathcal{P}_3\) be a short complex of presheaves of
4918	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. For a degree \(p\) and a tuple \(\sigma : \{0, \ldots, p\} \to \iota\)
4919	  write \(V_\sigma = \bigcap_k U(\sigma k)\) for the corresponding basic open. The \emph{per-face
4920	  short complex} \(\mathrm{face}(U, P, \sigma)\) is the short complex of abelian groups obtained by
4921	  evaluating \(P\) on sections over \(V_\sigma\),
4922	  \[
4923	    \mathcal{P}_1(V_\sigma) \longrightarrow \mathcal{P}_2(V_\sigma) \longrightarrow
4924	      \mathcal{P}_3(V_\sigma),
4925	  \]
4926	  the maps being the section-level actions over \(V_\sigma\) of the two maps of \(P\). It is the
4927	  \((p, \sigma)\)-indexed factor whose product over all tuples \(\sigma\) is the degree-\(p\) term
4928	  of the section {\v C}ech complex.
4929	\end{definition}
4930	
4931	\begin{definition}
4932	
4933	\leanok
4934	[Short complex of section {\v C}ech complexes]
4935	  \label{def:section_cech_short_complex}
4936	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechComplexShortComplex}
4937	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:section_cech_functoriality}
4938	  \textit{Project-local.}
4939	  For an index family \(U\) and a short complex
4940	  \(P : \mathcal{P}_1 \to \mathcal{P}_2 \to \mathcal{P}_3\) of presheaves of
4941	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, the \emph{short complex of section {\v C}ech complexes} is
4942	  \[
4943	    0 \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{P}_1) \to
4944	      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{P}_2) \to
4945	      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{P}_3) \to 0,
4946	  \]
4947	  a short complex in the category of cochain complexes of abelian groups, with the two maps the
4948	  chain maps induced by the maps of \(P\) through the functoriality of
4949	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_functoriality}. Its short-exactness is the conclusion of
4950	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_ses_of_basis} and the input to Lemma~\ref{lem:quotient_vanishing_cech}.
4951	\end{definition}
4952	
4953	\begin{definition}
4954	
4955	[Cover system on a basis]
4956	  \label{def:basis_cov_system}
4957	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.BasisCovSystem, AlgebraicGeometry.CovDatum}
4958	  \uses{def:cech_complex, lem:ses_cech_h1, lem:injective_cech_acyclic}
4959	  \textit{Project-bespoke encoding of the hypotheses of Stacks 01EO.}
4960	  A \emph{cover system on a basis} for a scheme \(X\) is a record with five fields. A
4961	  \emph{covering datum} (the helper \(\mathrm{CovDatum}\)) is a pair \((\iota, U)\) of an index type
4962	  \(\iota\) together with an index family \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\); it is the raw shape
4963	  \(\Sigma\,\iota,\ \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) over which the section {\v C}ech complex is built.
4964	  The cover system bundles:
4965	  \begin{enumerate}
4966	  \item a basis \(\mathcal{B} \subseteq \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) for the topology;
4967	  \item a set \(\mathrm{Cov}\) of admissible coverings, each a \(\mathrm{CovDatum}\);
4968	  \item \emph{faces-in-basis} (\(\mathrm{faces\_mem}\)): for every covering in \(\mathrm{Cov}\) every
4969	    finite intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) of its opens lies in \(\mathcal{B}\) (condition (1) of
4970	    Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis});
4971	  \item \emph{section surjectivity} (\(\mathrm{surj\_of\_vanishing}\)): for every \(V \in \mathcal{B}\)
4972	    and every short exact sequence \(S : 0 \to S_1 \to S_2 \to S_3 \to 0\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
4973	    whose left term \(S_1\) has vanishing positive {\v C}ech cohomology over the coverings of \(V\) in
4974	    \(\mathrm{Cov}\), the section map \(S_2(V) \to S_3(V)\) is surjective;
4975	  \item \emph{injective acyclicity} (\(\mathrm{injective\_acyclic}\)): for every injective
4976	    \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{I}\) and every covering \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathrm{Cov}\) the
4977	    positive-degree {\v C}ech cohomology vanishes, \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for
4978	    all \(p > 0\).
4979	  \end{enumerate}
4980	  Unlike a bare combinatorial record, the structure carries two sheaf-theoretic ({\v C}ech-cohomology)
4981	  fields. The field \(\mathrm{surj\_of\_vanishing}\) is \emph{not} the raw cofinality datum: it is the
4982	  section-surjectivity \emph{output} that cofinality together with Lemma~\ref{lem:ses_cech_h1}
4983	  produces, packaged directly as the consequence the 01EO chain consumes. The field
4984	  \(\mathrm{injective\_acyclic}\) is the injective {\v C}ech-acyclicity of
4985	  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} for the system's own coverings. These are exactly the data
4986	  that the dimension-shift induction Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_pos_vanishing} consumes.
4987	\end{definition}
4988	
4989	\begin{definition}
4990	
4991	\leanok
4992	[Vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for a cover system]
4993	  \label{def:has_vanishing_higher_cech}
4994	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.HasVanishingHigherCech}
4995	  \uses{def:basis_cov_system, def:cech_cohomology_accessor}
4996	  \textit{Project-bespoke inductive predicate (condition (3) of Stacks 01EO, per module).}
4997	  Given a cover system \(s\) on a basis (Definition~\ref{def:basis_cov_system}), an
4998	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{has vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for
4999	  \(s\)} when
5000	  \[
5001	    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad
5002	      \text{for every } \mathcal{U} \in s.\mathrm{Cov} \text{ and every } p > 0,
5003	  \]
5004	  with \(\check{H}^p\) the accessor of Definition~\ref{def:cech_cohomology_accessor}. This is the
5005	  per-module form of condition (3). It is deliberately stated for an \emph{arbitrary}
5006	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module: the dimension-shift induction of
5007	  Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_pos_vanishing} feeds the quotient \(\mathcal{Q} =
5008	  \mathcal{I}/\mathcal{F}\) back as the coefficient, and \(\mathcal{Q}\) need not be quasi-coherent
5009	  even when \(\mathcal{F}\) is. The inductive class is therefore ``\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules with
5010	  vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for \(s\)'', a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) entering only
5011	  as the seed at the affine (Tag 02KG) instantiation; the predicate must \emph{not} be specialized
5012	  to quasi-coherent modules.
5013	\end{definition}
5014	
5015	\begin{lemma}
5016	[Product of short exact sequences in \(\mathrm{Ab}\) (AB4*)]
5017	  \label{lem:short_exact_pi_map}
5018	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.shortExact_piMap, AlgebraicGeometry.pi_π_map_apply}
5019	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
5020	  \textit{Project AB4* lemma (Archon-original infrastructure).}
5021	  Let \((S_j)_{j \in J}\) be a family, indexed by an arbitrary type \(J\), of short exact sequences
5022	  of abelian groups, \(S_j : 0 \to A_j \xrightarrow{f_j} B_j \xrightarrow{g_j} C_j \to 0\). Then
5023	  the short complex assembled from the product maps,
5024	  \[
5025	    0 \to \prod_{j \in J} A_j \xrightarrow{\;\prod_j f_j\;} \prod_{j \in J} B_j
5026	      \xrightarrow{\;\prod_j g_j\;} \prod_{j \in J} C_j \to 0,
5027	  \]
5028	  is again short exact. This is the AB4* exactness-of-products property of \(\mathrm{Ab}\); it is
5029	  the degreewise engine of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_ses_of_basis}, since each {\v C}ech term is a product
5030	  of basic-open section groups (Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}).
5031	\end{lemma}
5032	\begin{proof}
5033	  Monomorphy of the product map \(\prod_j f_j\) is automatic, as products preserve monomorphisms.
5034	  Exactness at the middle is checked componentwise: an element of \(\prod_j B_j\) annihilated by
5035	  \(\prod_j g_j\) is annihilated in each coordinate \(j\), hence lies in the image of \(f_j\) by
5036	  exactness of \(S_j\), and the resulting coordinatewise preimages assemble to a preimage in
5037	  \(\prod_j A_j\). The one nontrivial point is epimorphy of \(\prod_j g_j\), which is \emph{not} an
5038	  automatic instance in \(\mathrm{Ab}\): given a target tuple in \(\prod_j C_j\), surjectivity of
5039	  each \(g_j\) supplies a coordinatewise preimage, and these assemble — through the canonical
5040	  identification of an \(\mathrm{Ab}\)-valued product with the set-theoretic product of its
5041	  components — to a preimage of the whole tuple. Hence \(\prod_j g_j\) is an epimorphism and the
5042	  displayed sequence is short exact.
5043	\end{proof}
5044	
5045	\begin{lemma}
5046	
5047	\leanok
5048	[Short exact sequence of {\v C}ech complexes from a basis]
5049	  \label{lem:cech_ses_of_basis}
5050	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechComplex_shortExact_of_basis}
5051	  \uses{def:cech_complex, def:face_short_complex, def:section_cech_short_complex,
5052	    lem:ses_cech_h1, lem:short_exact_pi_map}
5053	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Tag 01EO, Lemma
5054	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (proof, term-wise-product step) (read from
5055	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1695--1776).
5056	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "By Lemma \ref{lemma-ses-cech-h1} and our assumption (2)
5057	  % this sequence gives rise to an exact sequence
5058	  % $$
5059	  % 0 \to \mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{I}(U) \to \mathcal{Q}(U) \to 0.
5060	  % $$
5061	  % for every $U \in \mathcal{B}$. Hence for any $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$
5062	  % we get a short exact sequence of {\v C}ech complexes
5063	  % $$
5064	  % 0 \to
5065	  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \to
5066	  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) \to
5067	  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{Q}) \to 0
5068	  % $$
5069	  % since each term in the {\v C}ech complex is made up out of a product of
5070	  % values over elements of $\mathcal{B}$ by assumption (1)."
5071	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01EO,
5072	  \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (proof).}
5073	  Fix an index family \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) (giving the covering \(\mathcal{U}\)) and a
5074	  short complex
5075	  \[
5076	    P : \mathcal{P}_1 \to \mathcal{P}_2 \to \mathcal{P}_3
5077	  \]
5078	  of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Assume that for every degree \(p\) and every tuple
5079	  \(\sigma : \{0, \ldots, p\} \to \iota\) the per-face short complex of
5080	  Definition~\ref{def:face_short_complex} — the section sequence
5081	  \[
5082	    \mathcal{P}_1(V_\sigma) \longrightarrow \mathcal{P}_2(V_\sigma) \longrightarrow
5083	      \mathcal{P}_3(V_\sigma), \qquad V_\sigma = \bigcap_k U(\sigma k)
5084	  \]
5085	  — is short exact. Then the induced short complex of section {\v C}ech complexes of
5086	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_short_complex},
5087	  \[
5088	    0 \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{P}_1) \to
5089	      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{P}_2) \to
5090	      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{P}_3) \to 0,
5091	  \]
5092	  is short exact (as a short complex of cochain complexes of abelian groups).
5093	
5094	  \emph{Remark.} In the cover-global \((\mathcal{B}, \mathrm{Cov})\) setting of
5095	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}, where \(P\) is the section short complex of a short
5096	  exact sequence \(0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) of sheaves, the per-face
5097	  hypothesis is discharged by the per-face short-exact-sequence derivation of
5098	  Lemma~\ref{lem:face_ses_of_sheaf_ses}: left-exactness of the section sequence over \(V_\sigma\) is
5099	  automatic, and surjectivity \(\mathcal{I}(V_\sigma) \to \mathcal{Q}(V_\sigma)\) is the output of
5100	  Lemma~\ref{lem:ses_cech_h1} at the basic open \(V_\sigma\) (condition (1) placing \(V_\sigma \in
5101	  \mathcal{B}\)).
5102	\end{lemma}
5103	
5104	\begin{proof}
5105	  \uses{def:section_cech_short_complex, def:face_short_complex, lem:short_exact_pi_map}
5106	  \leanok
5107	  Short-exactness of a short complex of cochain complexes is checked degreewise: it suffices to
5108	  verify short-exactness in each cohomological degree \(p\). In degree \(p\) the term of the
5109	  section {\v C}ech complex is the product \(\prod_{\sigma}(-)(V_\sigma)\) over all tuples
5110	  \(\sigma : \{0, \ldots, p\} \to \iota\) of section groups over the basic opens \(V_\sigma\), and
5111	  the two maps are the products of the corresponding per-face maps; thus the degree-\(p\) short
5112	  complex is the product over \(\sigma\) of the per-face short complexes
5113	  (Definition~\ref{def:face_short_complex}). Each of these is short exact by hypothesis, so their
5114	  product is short exact by the AB4* product-of-short-exact-sequences lemma
5115	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:short_exact_pi_map}). As this holds in every degree, the short complex of section
5116	  {\v C}ech complexes (Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_short_complex}) is short exact.
5117	\end{proof}
5118	
5119	\begin{lemma}
5120	
5121	[Per-face short exact sequence from a sheaf short exact sequence]
5122	  \label{lem:face_ses_of_sheaf_ses}
5123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.faceShortComplex_shortExact_of_sheaf_ses,
5124	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionsFunctor}
5125	  \uses{lem:ses_cech_h1, def:face_short_complex}
5126	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Tag 01EO, Lemma
5127	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (proof, section-exactness step) (read from
5128	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1729--1734).
5129	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "By Lemma \ref{lemma-ses-cech-h1} and our assumption (2)
5130	  % this sequence gives rise to an exact sequence
5131	  % $$
5132	  % 0 \to \mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{I}(U) \to \mathcal{Q}(U) \to 0.
5133	  % $$
5134	  % for every $U \in \mathcal{B}$."
5135	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01EO,
5136	  \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (proof).}
5137	  Let \(S : 0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of
5138	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, let \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) be an index family,
5139	  and suppose that on every face \(V_\sigma = \bigcap_k U(\sigma k)\) the section map
5140	  \(\mathcal{I}(V_\sigma) \to \mathcal{Q}(V_\sigma)\) is surjective — the basis-surjectivity datum
5141	  supplied by Lemma~\ref{lem:ses_cech_h1}. Writing \(P\) for the section short complex of \(S\)
5142	  regarded as a short complex of presheaves of modules, the per-face short complex of
5143	  Definition~\ref{def:face_short_complex} is then short exact for every degree \(p\) and tuple
5144	  \(\sigma\). This lemma produces the per-face hypothesis consumed by
5145	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_ses_of_basis}, and is the bridge by which
5146	  Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_pos_vanishing} instantiates the cover-local lemmas from a
5147	  genuine sheaf short exact sequence.
5148	\end{lemma}
5149	\begin{proof}
5150	  \uses{lem:ses_cech_h1, def:face_short_complex}
5151	  Fix \(p\) and \(\sigma\), and put \(V = V_\sigma = \bigcap_k U(\sigma k)\). Monomorphy of
5152	  \(\mathcal{F}(V) \to \mathcal{I}(V)\) and exactness at the middle of \(\mathcal{F}(V) \to
5153	  \mathcal{I}(V) \to \mathcal{Q}(V)\) are the left-exactness of the sections functor applied to the
5154	  short exact sequence of sheaves \(S\): the sections functor \(\mathcal{G} \mapsto \mathcal{G}(V)\)
5155	  is the composite of the inclusion of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules into presheaves of
5156	  modules (a right adjoint, hence preserving finite limits) with evaluation at \(V\) (which
5157	  preserves limits), so it is left exact and carries the exactness of \(S\) to exactness of the
5158	  section sequence at \(\mathcal{I}(V)\). The remaining map \(\mathcal{I}(V) \to \mathcal{Q}(V)\) is
5159	  surjective by hypothesis, i.e. by the output of Lemma~\ref{lem:ses_cech_h1} at the basic open
5160	  \(V\). Hence the per-face short complex is short exact.
5161	\end{proof}

5186	  and \(T_1\) has vanishing positive homology. Therefore \(H^p(T_3) = 0\).

5316	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Since $\mathcal{F}$ was an arbitrary $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
